passages.explanation: Jesus says to his disciples: things that cause people to stumble are bound to come, but woe to anyone through whom they come. It would be better to have a millstone hung around your neck and be dropped into the sea than to cause one of these little ones to stumble. Watch yourselves. If your brother or sister sins against you, rebuke them. If they repent, forgive them. Even if they sin against you seven times in a day and seven times come back saying 'I repent,' you must forgive them. The severity of causing others to stumble is matched by the radicality of forgiveness. Seven times in one day — Jesus is describing a forgiveness that doesn't keep count.

The apostles say: increase our faith! The Lord replies: if you have faith as small as a mustard seed, you could say to this mulberry tree 'be uprooted and planted in the sea,' and it would obey you. Then he asks: suppose one of you has a servant ploughing or looking after the sheep. When the servant comes in from the field, do you say 'come along now and sit down to eat'? Won't you rather say 'prepare my supper, get yourself ready, and wait on me while I eat and drink'? Would you thank the servant because he did what he was told? So you also, when you've done everything you were told to do, should say: we are unworthy servants — we have only done our duty. The faith question gets an unexpected answer: it's not about quantity but about the nature of the relationship. Obedience isn't extraordinary — it's the baseline.

On the way to Jerusalem, Jesus travels along the border between Samaria and Galilee. As he enters a village, ten men with leprosy meet him. They stand at a distance and call out: Jesus, Master, have pity on us! He sees them and says: go, show yourselves to the priests. As they go, they are cleansed. One of them, when he sees he's healed, comes back praising God in a loud voice. He throws himself at Jesus' feet and thanks him — and he is a Samaritan. Jesus asks: were not all ten cleansed? Where are the other nine? Has no one returned to give praise to God except this foreigner? Then he says: rise and go — your faith has made you well. Ten are healed, one returns. And the one who returns is the outsider — the Samaritan. Luke keeps emphasising this pattern: the people you'd least expect are the ones who respond to God.

The Pharisees ask when the kingdom of God will come. Jesus replies: the coming of the kingdom of God is not something that can be observed, nor will people say 'here it is' or 'there it is,' because the kingdom of God is in your midst. Then he tells the disciples: the time is coming when you'll long to see one of the days of the Son of Man, but you won't see it. People will say 'there he is' or 'here he is' — don't go running after them. For the Son of Man in his day will be like the lightning that flashes and lights up the sky from one end to the other. But first he must suffer many things and be rejected by this generation. Just as it was in the days of Noah — people were eating, drinking, marrying, right up to the day Noah entered the ark, and the flood came and destroyed them all. It was the same in the days of Lot — eating, drinking, buying, selling, planting, building, until the day Lot left Sodom and fire and sulphur rained down and destroyed them all. It will be just like this on the day the Son of Man is revealed. On that day, don't go back for anything. Remember Lot's wife! Whoever tries to keep their life will lose it, and whoever loses their life will preserve it. Two people will be in one bed — one taken, the other left. Two women grinding grain — one taken, one left. The disciples ask: where, Lord? He replies: where there is a dead body, there the vultures will gather. The kingdom is already present in Jesus but its full revelation will be sudden and unmistakable — like lightning. No one will need to point it out.
